title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Relationships
meta_description: Trust your gut when evaluating relationships. This article shows you how to tell the difference between fear and true intuition, so you can stop second guessing yourself and make clear, confident choices.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Relationships
og_description: Trust your gut when evaluating relationships. This article shows you how to tell the difference between fear and true intuition, so you can stop second guessing yourself and make clear, confident choices.
body:
How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Relationships

You've been there. Sitting across from someone who looks good on paper. They check every box. Smart, stable, kind. Your friends love them. Your family approves. And yet, something in your gut is screaming. Not loud, not dramatic. Just a quiet, persistent "no" that won't shut up.

Or maybe it's the opposite. Everyone's telling you to run. They're listing red flags like they're reading a grocery list. But you feel something else. A pull. A knowing that doesn't make logical sense. And you can't explain it without sounding crazy.

That's your intuition. And if you're reading this, you already know it's real. You've just been taught not to trust it.

Here's the thing about being a deeply sensitive person with a wide-open nervous system - you pick up on things other people miss. You feel the energy in a room before anyone speaks. You know when someone's words don't match their vibe. You can sense when a relationship has an expiration date, even when everything looks fine from the outside.

And you've been told your whole life that something is wrong with you for feeling these things. That you're "too sensitive." That you're "overthinking." That you should "give people a chance."

Nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most. And learning to use that calibration when evaluating relationships isn't just helpful - it's survival.

Let's talk about how to actually do that. No fluff. No woo-woo that makes you feel like you need to be a guru. Just real, grounded ways to let your intuition guide you without getting lost in fear or fantasy.

Why Your Intuition Already Knows the Truth

Your intuition isn't magic. It's not some mystical power reserved for psychics and monks. It's your brain processing information faster than your conscious mind can keep up. Your nervous system picks up on micro-expressions, tone shifts, energy changes, and patterns you've seen before. It's running a background check on everyone you meet, and it doesn't need your permission to do it.

Think about the last time you met someone and immediately felt uneasy. You couldn't point to one thing they said or did. But you knew. Your body knew. Maybe your shoulders tensed. Maybe your stomach tightened. Maybe you felt a sudden urge to leave.

That wasn't anxiety. That was data.

The problem isn't that your intuition is wrong. The problem is that you've been trained to override it with logic, politeness, and social pressure. You've been told to ignore the knot in your stomach because "they seem nice." You've been taught that your feelings are unreliable, especially when they contradict what everyone else sees.

But here's the truth your intuition already knows: relationships are not about checking boxes. They're about resonance. They're about how someone makes your nervous system feel when you're around them. And your nervous system doesn't lie.

Your intuition isn't trying to protect you from what might happen. It's trying to show you what's already true. The only question is whether you're willing to listen before the evidence piles up.

When you start evaluating relationships through your intuition, you stop asking "Is this person good enough?" and start asking "How do I feel when I'm with this person?" That shift changes everything. Because someone can be good on paper and terrible for your nervous system. And someone can be imperfect and still feel like home.

Your intuition knows the difference. You just have to stop second-guessing yourself long enough to hear it.

The Body Knows Before Your Mind Does

Here's a practice I want you to try. Next time you're with someone you're evaluating - a date, a friend, a potential partner - check in with your body. Not your thoughts. Not your list of pros and cons. Just your body.

Where do you feel tension? Is your jaw tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Is your breathing shallow? Does your stomach feel like it's in a knot?

Now contrast that with how you feel when you're alone, or with someone who truly feels safe. Notice the difference.

Your body is giving you real-time feedback about every relationship you're in. Most people ignore it. They push through the tension because they want things to work. They override the signals because they're afraid of being alone, or they don't want to hurt someone's feelings, or they think they're being "too picky."

But here's what I've learned after years of working with sensitive people: the body never lies. It might be subtle. It might be easy to dismiss. But it's always accurate.

If you feel tight, closed, or contracted around someone, that's your intuition saying "this isn't safe." If you feel open, expansive, and relaxed, that's your intuition saying "this is okay." It's that simple. And it's that hard to trust.

One of the best books I've ever read on this is The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's not a psychic development book - it's about how your survival instincts work and why you should listen to them. The author explains that fear is a gift because it's your intuition screaming at you to pay attention. And the people who ignore that gift are the ones who end up in bad situations. See on Amazon (paid link)

Read that book if you want to understand why your body reacts the way it does. It'll change how you see every relationship you have.

Red Flags vs. Intuitive No's

There's a difference between a red flag and an intuitive "no." A red flag is something you can point to. They lied. They were rude to the waiter. They talked about their ex for an hour. Those are concrete things you can discuss with a friend and get validation for.

An intuitive "no" is harder to explain. You can't always point to one thing. It's a feeling. A knowing. A sense that something is off even when everything looks fine.

Both are valid. But the intuitive "no" is the one most people dismiss because they can't justify it to others. And that's where you get into trouble.

Here's the thing: your intuition doesn't need to prove itself to anyone. It doesn't need to be logical. It doesn't need to be explainable. It just needs to be heard.

If you feel a "no" in your body, that's enough. You don't need to find evidence. You don't need to wait for a red flag to validate the feeling. You can just honor it and move on.

This is hard for people who are used to being the "reasonable" one. The one who gives everyone the benefit of the doubt. The one who doesn't want to be "unfair." But here's the truth: honoring your intuition isn't being unfair to others. It's being fair to yourself. And you've been unfair to yourself for way too long.

If you struggle with trusting your intuitive "no's," I recommend picking up Rising Strong by Brené Brown. It's not about intuition specifically, but it's about the courage to trust yourself when the story doesn't make sense to anyone else. And that's exactly what you need when your intuition is telling you something your friends can't see. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Test Your Intuition Without Going Crazy

You don't have to trust your intuition blindly. In fact, you shouldn't. Intuition is a tool, not a god. It can get clouded by fear, trauma, and wishful thinking. The key is learning to distinguish between genuine intuition and the noise of your own stuff.

Here's a simple way to test it. When you're trying to evaluate a relationship, ask yourself this question: "If I had no fear of being alone, no fear of hurting their feelings, and no fear of what others would think, what would I choose?"

That question cuts through the noise. It separates your intuition from your fear. Because genuine intuition doesn't change based on circumstances. It's consistent. It's steady. It doesn't panic.

Fear, on the other hand, is reactive. It changes based on what's at stake. It gets louder when you're tired, hungry, or stressed. It tells you to stay in relationships that aren't good for you because the unknown is scarier than the familiar.

So when you're trying to figure out if your intuition is real, look for consistency. Does this feeling stay the same over time? Does it show up in your body the same way every time you're around this person? Does it feel more like a calm knowing or a frantic warning?

Calm knowing is intuition. Frantic warning is fear. Both are useful, but they require different responses.

Another way to test your intuition is to give yourself space. Don't make decisions in the middle of a conversation or right after a date. Give yourself 24 hours. Sleep on it. See how the feeling settles.

Genuine intuition gets clearer with time. Fear gets louder. If you're still feeling the same way after a good night's sleep and a quiet morning, that's worth paying attention to.

And if you really want to look closely at this, check out Psychic Witch by Mat Auryn. It's one of the most grounded books on developing your intuitive senses without losing your mind. It gives you actual practices to test and strengthen your abilities instead of just talking about them. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When Your Intuition Says "No" But Your Heart Says "Maybe"

This is the hardest one. When your intuition is giving you a clear "no," but there's a part of you that still wants it to work. Maybe you see potential. Maybe you're attached to the idea of who they could be. Maybe you're afraid of starting over.

Here's what I want you to understand: your intuition sees the person as they are right now. Your heart sees the person you want them to become. And those are two very different things.

Your intuition is grounded in present reality. It's picking up on the energy, the patterns, the subtle cues that tell you who this person actually is. Your heart is projecting into the future. It's imagining what could be if things were different.

When these two are in conflict, always trust your intuition. Always. Because the heart's projections are based on hope, not data. And hope is beautiful, but it's not a foundation for a relationship.

This doesn't mean you have to act immediately. You can take your time. You can gather more information. But don't ignore the "no" just because you want a different answer. That's how you end up in relationships that drain you for years.

I've seen it happen so many times. Someone feels the "no" early on. They ignore it because they don't want to be "judgmental" or "closed off." They give the person a chance. They invest time, energy, and love. And then, months or years later, the "no" proves itself right. And they're left wondering why they didn't listen sooner.

Don't be that person. You don't have to prove your intuition wrong just to avoid being "unfair." Your intuition is not your enemy. It's your most loyal friend. And it's trying to save you from a lot of pain.

The Practical Side: How to Use Intuition in Real-Time

Let's get practical. Here are some things you can actually do to use your intuition when evaluating relationships, starting today.

1. Check in before you meet up. Before a date or a hangout, close your eyes and ask yourself: "How do I feel about seeing this person?" Don't think about it. Just feel it. If you feel excitement or curiosity, that's good. If you feel dread, obligation, or numbness, that's data.

2. Notice how you feel after. This is huge. After you spend time with someone, check your energy. Do you feel drained or energized? Do you feel more like yourself or less? Do you feel peaceful or agitated? The answer tells you everything about the relationship's impact on your nervous system.

3. Pay attention to how they make you feel about yourself. This is the most underrated relationship metric. Do you feel smarter, funnier, and more alive around this person? Or do you feel smaller, quieter, and more anxious? The right relationship will make you feel like your best self. The wrong one will make you feel like you're too much or not enough.

4. Trust your first impression. Your first impression is your intuition's unfiltered read. Before your mind starts rationalizing, before you start making excuses, your body gives you a clean read. Write it down. Don't let your brain talk you out of it.

5. Use the "gut check" technique. When you're trying to make a decision about a relationship, put your hand on your stomach and ask a yes/no question. Notice what your body does. A "yes" usually feels open, expansive, or relaxed. A "no" usually feels tight, closed, or heavy. Practice this with small decisions first, and you'll get better at reading it with big ones.

When Your Intuition Is Clouded by Your Own Stuff

Let's be honest. Not every feeling you have is pure intuition. Sometimes what feels like intuition is actually fear, trauma, or old patterns talking. And learning to tell the difference is part of the work.

If you've been hurt before, your nervous system might be hypervigilant. It might flag people as unsafe when they're actually fine. It might confuse familiarity with danger because you grew up in chaos and safety feels boring or suspicious.

That's not intuition. That's a protective response that's out of date. And it needs to be healed, not followed.

Here's how to tell the difference. Intuition feels clear and grounded. It doesn't come with a story. It's just a knowing. Fear, on the other hand, comes with a narrative. It tells you "they're going to hurt you like the last one did" or "you can't trust anyone" or "something bad is going to happen." Intuition is quiet. Fear is loud.

If you're hearing a story, you're dealing with fear. If you're just feeling a knowing without the drama, that's intuition.

Healing your relationship with your own intuition means doing the work on your trauma. It means untangling what's yours from what's theirs. It means learning to trust yourself again after being let down by your own judgment in the past.

That's hard. But it's worth it. Because when you clear out the noise, your intuition becomes one of the most reliable tools you have for navigating relationships.

Final Thoughts: You Already Have What You Need

You don't need to be a psychic to use intuition in relationships. You don't need to meditate for hours or have visions or read auras. You just need to start listening to the signals your body is already sending you.

Your intuition is not a mystery. It's not something you have to earn or develop. It's already there, working all the time, trying to guide you toward what's good for you and away from what's not. The only thing standing between you and that guidance is your willingness to trust it.

So here's my challenge to you. For the next week, pay attention to your body's responses in your relationships. Notice when you feel tight and when you feel open. Notice when you feel drained and when you feel energized. Notice when you feel like yourself and when you feel like you're performing.

Write it down. Trust it. And see what happens.

You might be surprised at how much you already know. And how much easier life gets when you stop fighting your own knowing.

If you want to go deeper into this work, I've put together a free assessment that helps you identify where you're currently overriding your intuition in relationships and how to start trusting it again. Take the assessment here and see where you're at. It'll give you a clear starting point for the work ahead.
